updates.q: Logs vs metrics vs traces -- when to reach for each?
updates.a: Metrics: aggregate numeric time-series, cheap to store, drive alerts (is something wrong now?). Logs: discrete events, high-cardinality detail, answer 'why did this request fail?'. Traces: per-request causal graph across services, find the slow span. Use metrics to detect, traces to localize, logs to explain.

updates.q: GDPR data residency -- what does it actually require?
updates.a: EU personal data must stay in the EU unless the destination country has an adequacy decision or you use SCCs (Standard Contractual Clauses) plus a transfer impact assessment. Practical implication: run an EU region (Frankfurt, Ireland) and pin EU-tenant data to it; do not let logs/backups silently replicate to us-east-1.

updates.q: SCIM 2.0 -- the four endpoints that matter?
updates.a: POST /Users (create), PATCH /Users/{id} (update attributes), PATCH with active=false (deprovision/disable), and /Groups for role membership. The customer's IdP (Okta, Entra ID) calls these on a schedule so HR -> IdP -> your app stays in sync without manual user management.

new_cards.q: SAML vs OIDC vs OAuth 2.0 -- when do you reach for each?
new_cards.a: SAML: enterprise browser SSO into a SaaS app (XML assertions, IdP-initiated common). OIDC: modern SSO + identity (JWT id_token, mobile/SPA/API friendly, built on OAuth). OAuth 2.0: delegated authorization (access_token to call an API on a user's behalf) -- it is not authentication. Rule: SAML/OIDC answer 'who is the user?', OAuth answers 'what can this token do?'.

new_cards.q: Why is Authorization Code + PKCE the default in OAuth 2.1?
new_cards.a: PKCE (Proof Key for Code Exchange) binds the auth code to the original client via a code_verifier/code_challenge pair, defeating code interception on public clients (mobile, SPA). OAuth 2.1 made it mandatory for all clients -- public and confidential -- and removed Implicit and ROPC entirely.

new_cards.q: S3 vs GCS vs Azure Blob -- key differences?
new_cards.a: All three are object stores with 11 nines durability and lifecycle/IAM/encryption. S3 has the richest ecosystem (Athena, Glacier tiers, S3 Select) and strongest tooling. GCS has the cleanest consistency model and dual-region buckets. Azure Blob integrates tightly with AD/Entra and is mandatory in Microsoft-heavy enterprises. Pick by where the rest of your stack lives.

new_cards.q: PrivateLink vs VPN vs Direct Connect -- which when?
new_cards.a: PrivateLink (AWS) / Private Service Connect (GCP): expose a single service privately across VPCs/accounts without peering -- best for SaaS-to-customer. Site-to-site VPN: encrypted tunnel over the public internet, fast to set up, ~1 Gbps. Direct Connect / Cloud Interconnect: dedicated fiber, 10-100 Gbps, predictable latency, for big enterprise hybrid.

new_cards.q: IAM least-privilege -- what does 'good' look like in practice?
new_cards.a: Roles not users; one role per workload. Scope actions to specific resources (arn:aws:s3:::bucket/prefix/*), not '*'. Use session policies and permission boundaries to cap blast radius. Run Access Analyzer / IAM Recommender periodically to prune unused permissions. No long-lived access keys -- use IAM Roles for Service Accounts or Workload Identity.

new_cards.q: KMS key rotation -- automatic vs manual?
new_cards.a: AWS KMS auto-rotates symmetric CMKs yearly (no re-encrypt needed -- old key material stays for decrypt). Manual rotation is required for asymmetric keys and for customer-managed BYOK. Audit via CloudTrail kms:GenerateDataKey events. Rotating the data-encryption key (DEK) is cheap; rotating the master key (KEK) is rare and ceremonial.

new_cards.q: Secrets management -- Vault vs Secrets Manager vs Doppler?
new_cards.a: HashiCorp Vault: multi-cloud, dynamic secrets (short-lived DB creds), strongest model, ops-heavy. AWS Secrets Manager / GCP Secret Manager: cloud-native, IAM-integrated, automatic rotation hooks, cheap for single-cloud. Doppler/1Password Secrets: developer-friendly UX, good for app config across envs. Never commit secrets; use OIDC federation from CI so no static keys touch disk.

new_cards.q: CloudWatch vs Stackdriver vs Datadog -- tradeoffs?
new_cards.a: CloudWatch / Cloud Monitoring: cheapest, native, but weak cross-account UX and slow dashboards. Datadog: best dashboards, APM, and unified logs+metrics+traces, but cost scales painfully past ~100 hosts. Honeycomb / Grafana Cloud: strong on traces and high-cardinality. Rule: start native, move to Datadog when you have on-call rotations, build out OTel so the backend choice stays portable.

new_cards.q: SLI vs SLO vs SLA -- the precise definitions?
new_cards.a: SLI: the measurement (e.g., 'fraction of requests with latency < 300ms'). SLO: the internal target (e.g., 'SLI >= 99.9% over 30 days'). SLA: the customer contract with penalties (e.g., '99.5% or 10% credit'). SLO is always tighter than SLA. Error budget = 1 - SLO; when burned, freeze risky deploys.

new_cards.q: Terraform vs CDK -- when to pick which?
new_cards.a: Terraform: declarative HCL, multi-cloud, huge module ecosystem, state file is the source of truth -- great for infra teams managing many accounts. CDK (AWS/CDKTF): write infra in TypeScript/Python with loops/conditionals -- great for app teams who want infra colocated with code. CDK compiles to CloudFormation/Terraform under the hood. Default to Terraform for org-wide platforms, CDK for product squads.

new_cards.q: Autoscaling triggers -- which metric for which workload?
new_cards.a: Stateless web/API: target CPU 60-70% or requests-per-target (ALB). Queue workers: scale on queue depth / age of oldest message (SQS ApproximateAgeOfOldestMessage). GPU inference: scale on in-flight request count or token-throughput, not CPU. Avoid memory-based scaling for JVM/Python -- they hoard heap and never scale down.

new_cards.q: Lambda / Cloud Run cold-start mitigation -- the playbook?
new_cards.a: (1) Provisioned concurrency / min-instances = 1 for latency-critical paths. (2) Trim the deploy artifact -- bundle, tree-shake, drop SDKs you do not use. (3) Lazy-load heavy deps after the handler returns its first byte. (4) Prefer SnapStart (Lambda) or container CPU-always-allocated (Cloud Run gen2). (5) Move to a long-running container if p99 cold-start dominates.

new_cards.q: Multi-region failover -- active-active vs active-passive?
new_cards.a: Active-passive: primary serves traffic, standby replicates async, Route53/Cloud DNS health-check failover. RTO minutes, RPO seconds-to-minutes, cheap. Active-active: both regions serve, requires conflict-free writes (CRDTs, region-pinned tenants, or global DB like Spanner/DynamoDB Global Tables). RTO near zero, RPO near zero, 2x cost and much harder consistency story.

new_cards.q: Eventually-consistent reads -- when do they bite you?
new_cards.a: Read-after-write on a different replica returns stale data (DynamoDB eventually-consistent reads, S3 cross-region replication, read replicas). Bites you on: 'create resource then immediately GET it', dashboard 'undo' flows, and balance/quota checks. Fix: strong/consistent read for the immediate follow-up, or write-through cache keyed by request id.

new_cards.q: SOC 2 Type I vs Type II -- the difference enterprise buyers care about?
new_cards.a: Type I: a point-in-time snapshot -- controls were designed correctly on date X. Type II: controls operated effectively over a period (typically 6-12 months) with auditor sampling. Enterprise procurement almost always demands Type II; Type I is acceptable only as a bridge in year one. The five Trust Services Criteria: Security (always), Availability, Confidentiality, Processing Integrity, Privacy (pick based on product).

new_cards.q: HIPAA -- what does 'covered' actually require for a SaaS vendor?
new_cards.a: Sign a BAA (Business Associate Agreement) with the covered entity. Implement Administrative, Physical, and Technical safeguards: access controls (unique user IDs, auto-logoff), audit logs of PHI access, encryption at rest and in transit, breach notification within 60 days. Use a HIPAA-eligible service list (AWS publishes one); a single non-eligible service in the data path breaks compliance.

new_cards.q: FedRAMP Moderate vs High -- when do you need which?
new_cards.a: FedRAMP Moderate: most federal SaaS (CUI, ~325 controls from NIST 800-53). FedRAMP High: law enforcement, emergency services, financial systems -- ~410 controls plus stricter background checks and US-citizen operator requirements. Practical path: build on AWS GovCloud or Azure Government, inherit their ATO, get a 3PAO audit. Authorization timeline is 12-18 months and ~$1-2M minimum.

new_cards.q: VPC peering vs Transit Gateway -- when to graduate?
new_cards.a: VPC peering: 1:1, non-transitive (A-B and B-C does not give A-C), free data transfer in-region -- fine for 2-5 VPCs. Transit Gateway / Network Connectivity Center: hub-and-spoke, transitive routing, supports VPN and Direct Connect attachments -- mandatory once you have 10+ VPCs or multi-account org. Cost is per-attachment per-hour plus data; budget for it.

new_cards.q: Service mesh basics -- Istio vs Linkerd?
new_cards.a: Both inject a sidecar proxy that handles mTLS, retries, circuit-breaking, and L7 telemetry without app code changes. Istio: feature-rich (Envoy-based, WASM extensions, traffic mirroring), heavier control plane, steep ops cost. Linkerd: Rust-based 'micro-proxy', simpler, lower CPU/memory overhead, fewer knobs. Default to Linkerd for most teams; pick Istio only if you need its specific traffic-policy features.

new_cards.q: Webhook idempotency -- the receiver-side pattern?
new_cards.a: Sender includes a stable event_id (UUID) per logical event and retries with the same id. Receiver stores processed event_ids in a dedupe table with a TTL longer than the sender's retry window (24-48h). On replay, look up the id: if seen, return 200 immediately without re-running side effects. Combine with HMAC signature + timestamp tolerance (~5 min) to block replays from attackers.

new_cards.q: Terraform state -- why remote backend + state locking matter?
new_cards.a: State file holds the mapping from HCL to real resource IDs plus secrets. Local state breaks team workflows and leaks creds into git. Remote backend (S3+DynamoDB, GCS, Terraform Cloud) gives shared state, encryption at rest, and a lock so two engineers cannot 'apply' concurrently and corrupt the file. Always enable versioning on the backend bucket so you can roll back a botched apply.
